next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

She started with the oldest messages.

It was Sunday, the morning after. Nora sat on her bedroom floor with her back against the bed and her phone in both hands and the radiator clicking the way it did when the building's heat came on, which it shouldn't have — it was late March, the super had supposedly switched to the spring schedule, but old buildings ran on their own calendars and no one had told the boiler that winter was finished. The heat was wrong for the season and the room was too warm and she was still wearing the clothes she'd come home in last night, the jeans and the striped shirt she'd put on to go to Simone's apartment for Thai food, which she had eaten, all of it, every bite of the pad see ew and two of the summer rolls and the mango sticky rice, because she had been performing the role of a woman who did not know, and performing had apparently included appetite.

She opened WhatsApp and scrolled to Caleb Mora's thread and went all the way back to the beginning.

The first message was from October. Five months ago. He'd responded to her Hinge prompt — the one about the last book that made her cry — and she'd moved the conversation to WhatsApp within a day because the Hinge interface felt too formal, too transactional, and she'd wanted the intimacy of a messaging app where she could see when he was typing. She remembered that desire. She remembered the little pulse of courage it had taken to suggest the move, as if proposing a change of venue on a date that was going well.

*What was the last book that actually wrecked you?* That was his openin...
open_hooks_to_carry: Simone leaves to pick up food — Nora opens the laptop — the OpenClaw dashboard
Nora sees the conversation log, the sentiment analysis, the prompt engineering notes, the financial tracking
prev_tail_excerpt:
...side this relationship for five months without asking that question. She had loved him without it, sent money without it, sent a photograph of her body to him without it. Now the question was in the room — sitting in the chair across from her, patient and quiet, impossible to answer — because the evidence pointed in both directions and her heart pointed in one and her head was beginning, slowly, to point in the other, and the space between them was the exact shape of a life she had built on hope and was not yet ready to take apart.

She lay down on her bed as the light outside went grey and flat and listened to the building settle around her — the pipes, the muffled television, a siren working its way west on Atlantic Avenue. She did not text Caleb again. She did not open WhatsApp or Hinge or any of the apps that had, over five months, become the infrastructure of a love she might have built herself, or that might have been built for her, or that might be real. She lay in the early dark with the doubt and the love together in her chest, neither one leaving, neither one winning, and she understood that this was the new shape of things — not knowing, and going on anyway, and the going on not feeling like courage or foolishness but like the only direction left that didn't require her to become someone she wasn't ready to be.

Outside, March did what March does in Brooklyn — couldn't decide, wouldn't commit, hung between what it had been and what it was becoming, the last ice on the sidewalks going grey and soft in the gutters, the season not yet arrived but no longer deniable.
state_deltas_required: The truth, encountered accidentally and understood instantly
Nora knows everything — the power dynamic between the two women inverts completely
